is a [mobile device](/wiki/Mobile_device "Mobile device") that combines the functionality of a traditional [mobile phone](/wiki/Mobile_phone "Mobile phone") with advanced [computing](/wiki/Computing "Computing") capabilities. It typically has a [touchscreen](/wiki/Touchscreen "Touchscreen") interface, allowing users to access a wide range of applications and services, such as [web browsing](/wiki/Web_browsing "Web browsing"), [email](/wiki/Email "Email"), and [social media](/wiki/Social_media "Social media"), as well as [multimedia](/wiki/Multimedia "Multimedia") playback and [streaming](/wiki/Streaming_media "Streaming media"). Smartphones have built-in [cameras](/wiki/Camera "Camera"), [GPS navigation](/wiki/GPS_navigation "GPS navigation"), and support for various communication methods, including voice calls, [text messaging](/wiki/Text_messaging "Text messaging"), and internet-based messaging apps. Smartphones are distinguished from older-design [feature phones](/wiki/Feature_phone "Feature phone") by their more advanced hardware capabilities and extensive [mobile operating systems](/wiki/Mobile_operating_system "Mobile operating system"), access to the [internet](/wiki/Internet "Internet"), business applications, [mobile payments](/wiki/Mobile_payment "Mobile payment"), and multimedia functionality, including music, video, [gaming](/wiki/Mobile_gaming "Mobile gaming"), [radio](/wiki/Internet_radio "Internet radio"), and [television](/wiki/Mobile_television "Mobile television").

A **smartphone** is a [mobile device](/wiki/Mobile_device "Mobile device") that combines the functionality of a traditional [mobile phone](/wiki/Mobile_phone "Mobile phone") with advanced [computing](/wiki/Computing "Computing") capabilities. It typically has a [touchscreen](/wiki/Touchscreen "Touchscreen") interface, allowing users to access a wide range of applications and services, such as [web browsing](/wiki/Web_browsing "Web browsing"), [email](/wiki/Email "Email"), and [social media](/wiki/Social_media "Social media"), as well as [multimedia](/wiki/Multimedia "Multimedia") playback and [streaming](/wiki/Streaming_media "Streaming media"). Smartphones have built-in [cameras](/wiki/Camera "Camera"), [GPS navigation](/wiki/GPS_navigation "GPS navigation"), and support for various communication methods, including voice calls, [text messaging](/wiki/Text_messaging "Text messaging"), and internet-based messaging apps. Smartphones are distinguished from older-design [feature phones](/wiki/Feature_phone "Feature phone") by their more advanced hardware capabilities and extensive [mobile operating systems](/wiki/Mobile_operating_system "Mobile operating system"), access to the [internet](/wiki/Internet "Internet"), business applications, [mobile payments](/wiki/Mobile_payment "Mobile payment"), and multimedia functionality, including music, video, [gaming](/wiki/Mobile_gaming "Mobile gaming"), [radio](/wiki/Internet_radio "Internet radio"), and [television](/wiki/Mobile_television "Mobile television").

Smartphones typically feature [metal–oxide–semiconductor](/wiki/MOSFET "MOSFET") (MOS) [integrated circuit](/wiki/Integrated_circuit "Integrated circuit") (IC) chips, various sensors, and support for multiple [wireless](/wiki/Wireless "Wireless") communication protocols. Examples of smartphone sensors include [accelerometers](/wiki/Accelerometer "Accelerometer"), [barometers](/wiki/Barometer "Barometer"), [gyroscopes](/wiki/Gyroscope "Gyroscope"), and [magnetometers](/wiki/Magnetometer "Magnetometer"); they can be used by both pre-installed and third-party software to enhance functionality. Wireless communication standards supported by smartphones include [LTE](/wiki/LTE_(telecommunication) "LTE (telecommunication)"), [5G NR](/wiki/5G_NR "5G NR"), [Wi-Fi](/wiki/Wi-Fi "Wi-Fi"), [Bluetooth](/wiki/Bluetooth "Bluetooth"), and [satellite navigation](/wiki/Satellite_navigation "Satellite navigation"). By the mid-2020s, manufacturers began integrating [satellite messaging](/wiki/Satellite_phone#Integration_into_conventional_mobile_phones "Satellite phone") and emergency services, expanding their utility in remote areas without reliable [cellular coverage](/wiki/Cellular_coverage "Cellular coverage"). Smartphones have largely replaced [personal digital assistant](/wiki/Personal_digital_assistant "Personal digital assistant") (PDA) devices, [handheld/palm-sized PCs](/wiki/Handheld_PC "Handheld PC"), [portable media players](/wiki/Portable_media_player "Portable media player") (PMP),[[1]](#cite_note-Tom's_Hardware-1) [point-and-shoot cameras](/wiki/Point-and-shoot_camera "Point-and-shoot camera"), [camcorders](/wiki/Camcorder "Camcorder"), and, to a lesser extent, [handheld video game consoles](/wiki/Handheld_game_console "Handheld game console"), [e-reader](/wiki/E-reader "E-reader") devices, [pocket calculators](/wiki/Pocket_calculator "Pocket calculator"), and [GPS tracking units](/wiki/GPS_tracking_unit "GPS tracking unit").

Following the rising popularity of the [iPhone](/wiki/IPhone "IPhone") in the late 2000s, the majority of smartphones have featured thin, slate-like [form factors](/wiki/Form_factor_(mobile_phones) "Form factor (mobile phones)") with large, [capacitive](/wiki/Capacitive_sensing "Capacitive sensing") touch screens with support for [multi-touch](/wiki/Multi-touch "Multi-touch") gestures rather than physical keyboards. Most modern smartphones have the ability for users to download or purchase additional [applications](/wiki/Mobile_app "Mobile app") from a centralized [app store](/wiki/App_store "App store"). They often have support for [cloud storage](/wiki/Mobile_cloud_storage "Mobile cloud storage") and cloud synchronization, and [virtual assistants](/wiki/Virtual_assistant "Virtual assistant"). Since the early 2010s, improved hardware and faster wireless communication have [bolstered the growth of the smartphone industry](/wiki/Post-PC_era "Post-PC era"). As of 2014[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Smartphone&action=edit), over a billion smartphones are sold globally every year. In 2019 alone, 1.54 billion smartphone units were shipped worldwide.[[2]](#cite_note-2) As of 2020[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Smartphone&action=edit), 75.05 percent of the world population were smartphone users.[[3]](#cite_note-3)

## Hardware

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/91/Samsung_Galaxy_S6_edge_%2817448278539%29.jpg/250px-Samsung_Galaxy_S6_edge_%2817448278539%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Samsung_Galaxy_S6_edge_(17448278539).jpg)

Smartphone with infrared transmitter on top for use as remote control

Further information: [Mobile phone feature](/wiki/Mobile_phone_feature "Mobile phone feature")

A typical smartphone contains a number of [metal–oxide–semiconductor](/wiki/Metal%E2%80%93oxide%E2%80%93semiconductor "Metal–oxide–semiconductor") (MOS) [integrated circuit](/wiki/Integrated_circuit "Integrated circuit") (IC) chips,[[4]](#cite_note-Kim-4) which in turn contain billions of tiny [MOS field-effect transistors](/wiki/MOSFET "MOSFET") (MOSFETs).[[5]](#cite_note-uspto-5) A typical smartphone contains the following MOS IC chips:[[4]](#cite_note-Kim-4)

* [Application processor](/wiki/Microprocessor "Microprocessor") ([CMOS](/wiki/CMOS "CMOS") [system-on-a-chip](/wiki/System-on-a-chip "System-on-a-chip"))
* [Flash memory](/wiki/Flash_memory "Flash memory") ([floating-gate](/wiki/Floating-gate "Floating-gate") [MOS memory](/wiki/MOS_memory "MOS memory"))
* [Cellular modem](/wiki/Cellular_modem "Cellular modem") ([baseband](/wiki/Baseband "Baseband") [RF CMOS](/wiki/RF_CMOS "RF CMOS"))
* [RF transceiver](/wiki/RF_transceiver "RF transceiver") ([RF CMOS](/wiki/RF_CMOS "RF CMOS"))
* [Phone camera](/wiki/Phone_camera "Phone camera") [image sensor](/wiki/Image_sensor "Image sensor") ([CMOS image sensor](/wiki/CMOS_image_sensor "CMOS image sensor"))
* [Power management integrated circuit](/wiki/Power_management_integrated_circuit "Power management integrated circuit") ([power MOSFETs](/wiki/Power_MOSFET "Power MOSFET"))
* [Display driver](/wiki/Display_driver "Display driver") ([LCD](/wiki/LCD "LCD") or [LED driver](/wiki/LED_driver "LED driver"))
* [Wireless communication](/wiki/Wireless_communication "Wireless communication") chips ([Wi-Fi](/wiki/Wi-Fi "Wi-Fi"), [Bluetooth](/wiki/Bluetooth "Bluetooth"), [GPS receiver](/wiki/GPS_receiver "GPS receiver"))
* [Sound chip](/wiki/Sound_chip "Sound chip") ([audio codec](/wiki/Audio_codec "Audio codec") and [power amplifier](/wiki/Power_amplifier "Power amplifier"))
* [Gyroscope](/wiki/Gyroscope "Gyroscope")
* [Capacitive](/wiki/Capacitive_sensing "Capacitive sensing") [touchscreen](/wiki/Touchscreen "Touchscreen") controller ([ASIC](/wiki/ASIC "ASIC") and [DSP](/wiki/Digital_signal_processor "Digital signal processor"))[[4]](#cite_note-Kim-4)[[6]](#cite_note-6)[[7]](#cite_note-7)
* [RF power amplifier](/wiki/RF_power_amplifier "RF power amplifier") ([LDMOS](/wiki/LDMOS "LDMOS"))[[8]](#cite_note-Baliga2005-8)[[9]](#cite_note-Asif-9)[[10]](#cite_note-NXP-LDMOS-10)

Some are also equipped with an [FM radio](/wiki/FM_radio "FM radio") receiver, a hardware [notification LED](/wiki/Notification_LED "Notification LED"), and an infrared transmitter for use as [remote control](/wiki/Remote_control "Remote control"). A few models have additional [sensors](#Sensors) such as [thermometer](/wiki/Thermometer "Thermometer") for measuring ambient temperature, [hygrometer](/wiki/Hygrometer "Hygrometer") for humidity, and a sensor for [ultraviolet ray](/wiki/Ultraviolet_ray "Ultraviolet ray") measurement.

A few smartphones designed around specific purposes are equipped with uncommon hardware such as a projector ([Samsung Beam i8520](/wiki/Samsung_Beam_i8520 "Samsung Beam i8520") and [Samsung Galaxy Beam i8530](/wiki/Samsung_Galaxy_Beam_i8530 "Samsung Galaxy Beam i8530")), [optical zoom lenses](/wiki/Optical_zoom_lenses "Optical zoom lenses") ([Samsung Galaxy S4 Zoom](/wiki/Samsung_Galaxy_S4_Zoom "Samsung Galaxy S4 Zoom") and [Samsung Galaxy K Zoom](/wiki/Samsung_Galaxy_K_Zoom "Samsung Galaxy K Zoom")), [thermal camera](/wiki/Thermal_camera "Thermal camera"), and even [PMR446](/wiki/PMR446 "PMR446") ([walkie-talkie](/wiki/Walkie-talkie "Walkie-talkie") radio) [transceiver](/wiki/Transceiver "Transceiver").[[11]](#cite_note-11)[[12]](#cite_note-12)

### Central processing unit

Smartphones have [central processing units](/wiki/Central_processing_unit "Central processing unit") (CPUs), similar to those in computers, but optimised to operate in low power environments. In smartphones, the CPU is typically integrated in a [CMOS](/wiki/CMOS "CMOS") (complementary [metal–oxide–semiconductor](/wiki/Metal%E2%80%93oxide%E2%80%93semiconductor "Metal–oxide–semiconductor")) [system-on-a-chip](/wiki/System-on-a-chip "System-on-a-chip") (SoC) [application processor](/wiki/Microprocessor "Microprocessor").[[4]](#cite_note-Kim-4)

The performance of mobile CPU depends not only on the clock rate (generally given in multiples of [hertz](/wiki/Hertz "Hertz"))[[13]](#cite_note-13) but also on the [memory hierarchy](/wiki/Memory_hierarchy "Memory hierarchy"). Because of these challenges, the performance of mobile phone CPUs is often more appropriately given by scores derived from various standardized tests to measure the real effective performance in commonly used applications.

### Buttons

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a1/TouchWiz_2013_device_options.png/250px-TouchWiz_2013_device_options.png)](/wiki/File:TouchWiz_2013_device_options.png)

"Device options" menu of Samsung Mobile's [TouchWiz](/wiki/TouchWiz "TouchWiz") user interface as of 2013, accessed by holding the power button for a second

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/9e/HTC_Desire_-_optic_navigation.jpeg/250px-HTC_Desire_-_optic_navigation.jpeg)](/wiki/File:HTC_Desire_-_optic_navigation.jpeg)

The [HTC Desire](/wiki/HTC_Desire "HTC Desire"), a 2010 smartphone with [optical trackpad](/wiki/Optical_trackpad "Optical trackpad") and search button

Smartphones are typically equipped with a power button and volume buttons. Some pairs of volume buttons are unified. Some are equipped with a dedicated camera shutter button. Units for outdoor use may be equipped with an "SOS" emergency call and "PTT" ([push-to-talk](/wiki/Push-to-talk "Push-to-talk") button). The presence of physical front-side buttons such as the [home](/w/index.php?title=Home_button&action=edit&redlink=1 "Home button (page does not exist)") and navigation buttons has decreased throughout the 2010s, increasingly becoming replaced by capacitive touch sensors and simulated (on-screen) buttons.[[14]](#cite_note-14)

As with classic mobile phones, early smartphones such as the [Samsung Omnia II](/wiki/Samsung_Omnia_II "Samsung Omnia II") were equipped with buttons for accepting and declining phone calls. Due to the advancements of functionality besides phone calls, these have increasingly been replaced by navigation buttons such as "menu" (also known as "options"), "back", and "tasks". Some early 2010s smartphones such as the [HTC Desire](/wiki/HTC_Desire "HTC Desire") were additionally equipped with a "Search" button (🔍) for quick access to a web search engine or apps' internal search feature.[[15]](#cite_note-15)

Since 2013, smartphones' home buttons started integrating [fingerprint scanners](/wiki/Fingerprint_scanner "Fingerprint scanner"), starting with the [iPhone 5s](/wiki/IPhone_5s "IPhone 5s") and [Samsung Galaxy S5](/wiki/Samsung_Galaxy_S5 "Samsung Galaxy S5").

Functions may be assigned to button combinations. For example, [screenshots](/wiki/Screenshot "Screenshot") can usually be taken using the home and power buttons, with a short press on iOS and one-second holding Android OS, the two most popular mobile operating systems. On smartphones with no physical home button, usually the volume-down button is instead pressed with the power button. Some smartphones have a screenshot and possibly [screencast](/wiki/Screencast "Screencast") shortcuts in the navigation button bar or the power button menu.[[16]](#cite_note-16)[[17]](#cite_note-17)[[18]](#cite_note-18)

### Display

Main article: [Display device](/wiki/Display_device "Display device")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/0e/Livraria_do_Senado_%2822622160063%29.jpg/250px-Livraria_do_Senado_%2822622160063%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Livraria_do_Senado_(22622160063).jpg)

A smartphone touchscreen

One of the main characteristics of smartphones is the [screen](/wiki/Display_device "Display device"). Depending on the device's design, the screen fills most or nearly all of the space on a device's front surface. Many smartphone displays have an [aspect ratio](/wiki/Display_aspect_ratio "Display aspect ratio") of [16:9](/wiki/16:9 "16:9"), but taller aspect ratios became more common in 2017, as well as the aim to eliminate bezels by extending the display surface to as close to the edges as possible.

#### Screen sizes

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/12/Blackview_A60_Smartphone_Android_mobile_phone_front_face_logged_in_screen.jpg/250px-Blackview_A60_Smartphone_Android_mobile_phone_front_face_logged_in_screen.jpg)](/wiki/File:Blackview_A60_Smartphone_Android_mobile_phone_front_face_logged_in_screen.jpg)

Blackview A60 Andoid Go mobile phone smartphone cell phone showing the front-facing camera is put in notch display

Screen sizes are measured in diagonal [inches](/wiki/Inch "Inch"). Phones with screens larger than 5.2 inches are often called "[phablets](/wiki/Phablet "Phablet")". Smartphones with screens over 4.5 inches in size are commonly difficult to use with only a single hand, since most thumbs cannot reach the entire screen surface; they may need to be shifted around in the hand, held in one hand and manipulated by the other, or used in place with both hands. Due to design advances, some modern smartphones with large screen sizes and "edge-to-edge" designs have compact builds that improve their ergonomics, while the shift to taller aspect ratios have resulted in phones that have larger screen sizes whilst maintaining the ergonomics associated with smaller 16:9 displays.[[19]](#cite_note-phonearena-s7edgenotphablet-19)[[20]](#cite_note-verge-phabletpytha-20)[[21]](#cite_note-verge-s8aspectratio-21)

#### Panel types

[Liquid-crystal displays](/wiki/Liquid-crystal_display "Liquid-crystal display") (LCDs) and [organic light-emitting diode](/wiki/Organic_light-emitting_diode "Organic light-emitting diode") (OLED) displays are the most common. Some displays are integrated with pressure-sensitive digitizers, such as those developed by [Wacom](/wiki/Wacom_(company) "Wacom (company)") and [Samsung](/wiki/Samsung "Samsung"),[[22]](#cite_note-22) and Apple's [Force Touch](/wiki/Force_Touch "Force Touch") system. A few phones, such as the [YotaPhone](/wiki/YotaPhone "YotaPhone") prototype, are equipped with a low-power [electronic paper](/wiki/Electronic_paper "Electronic paper") rear display, as used in [e-book readers](/wiki/E-book_reader "E-book reader").

#### Alternative input methods

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b2/Mobile_URL_tooltip.png/250px-Mobile_URL_tooltip.png)](/wiki/File:Mobile_URL_tooltip.png)

Tooltip in Kiwi Browser, a [Google Chromium](/wiki/Google_Chromium "Google Chromium") derivative, reveals the full URL by hovering over the [tab](/wiki/Tabbed_browsing "Tabbed browsing") list using the stylus on a [Samsung Galaxy Note 4](/wiki/Samsung_Galaxy_Note_4 "Samsung Galaxy Note 4").

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e3/HTC_Legend_-_optic_sensor.jpeg/250px-HTC_Legend_-_optic_sensor.jpeg)](/wiki/File:HTC_Legend_-_optic_sensor.jpeg)

Optical track pad sensor of an [HTC Legend](/wiki/HTC_Legend "HTC Legend"), 2010.

Some devices are equipped with additional input methods such as a [stylus](/wiki/Stylus "Stylus") for higher precision input and hovering detection or a [self-capacitive touch screens](/wiki/Touchscreen#Self-capacitance "Touchscreen") layer for floating finger detection. The latter has been implemented on few phones such as the [Samsung Galaxy S4](/wiki/Samsung_Galaxy_S4 "Samsung Galaxy S4"), [Note 3](/wiki/Samsung_Galaxy_Note_3 "Samsung Galaxy Note 3"), [S5](/wiki/Samsung_Galaxy_S5 "Samsung Galaxy S5"), [Alpha](/wiki/Samsung_Galaxy_Alpha "Samsung Galaxy Alpha"), and [Sony Xperia Sola](/wiki/Sony_Xperia_Sola "Sony Xperia Sola"), making the Galaxy Note 3 the only smartphone with both so far.

Hovering can enable preview [tooltips](/wiki/Tooltip "Tooltip") such as on the [video player](/wiki/Video_player "Video player")'s seek bar, in text messages, and quick contacts on the [dial pad](/wiki/Dial_pad "Dial pad"), as well as [lock screen](/wiki/Lock_screen "Lock screen") animations, and the simulation of a [hovering](/wiki/Mouseover "Mouseover") [mouse cursor](/wiki/Mouse_cursor "Mouse cursor") on web sites.[[23]](#cite_note-23)[[24]](#cite_note-24)[[25]](#cite_note-25)

Some styluses support hovering as well and are equipped with a button for quick access to relevant tools such as digital [post-it notes](/wiki/Post-it_note "Post-it note") and highlighting of text and elements when dragging while pressed, resembling drag selection using a [computer mouse](/wiki/Computer_mouse "Computer mouse"). Some series such as the [Samsung Galaxy Note series](/wiki/Samsung_Galaxy_Note_series "Samsung Galaxy Note series") and [LG G Stylus](/w/index.php?title=LG_G_Stylus&action=edit&redlink=1 "LG G Stylus (page does not exist)") series have an integrated tray to store the stylus in.[[26]](#cite_note-26)

Few devices such as the [iPhone 6s](/wiki/IPhone_6s "IPhone 6s") until [iPhone Xs](/wiki/IPhone_Xs "IPhone Xs") and [Huawei Mate S](/wiki/Huawei_Mate_S "Huawei Mate S") are equipped with a [pressure-sensitive touch screen](/wiki/Force_Touch "Force Touch"), where the pressure may be used to simulate a gas pedal in video games, access to preview windows and shortcut menus, controlling the typing cursor, and a weight scale, the latest of which has been rejected by Apple from the [App Store](/wiki/App_Store_(iOS/iPadOS) "App Store (iOS/iPadOS)").[[27]](#cite_note-27)[[28]](#cite_note-28)

Some early 2010s HTC smartphones such as the [HTC Desire (Bravo)](/wiki/HTC_Desire "HTC Desire") and [HTC Legend](/wiki/HTC_Legend "HTC Legend") are equipped with an optical track pad for scrolling and selection.[[29]](#cite_note-29)

#### Notification light



Main article: [Notification LED](/wiki/Notification_LED "Notification LED")

Many smartphones except Apple iPhones are equipped with low-power [light-emitting diodes](/wiki/Light-emitting_diode "Light-emitting diode") besides the screen that are able to notify the user about incoming messages, missed calls, low battery levels, and facilitate locating the mobile phone in darkness, with marginial power consumption.

To distinguish between the sources of notifications, the colour combination and blinking pattern can vary. Usually three diodes in red, green, and blue ([RGB](/wiki/RGB "RGB")) are able to create a multitude of colour combinations.

### Sensors

Smartphones are equipped with a multitude of sensors to enable system features and third-party applications.

#### Common sensors

[Accelerometers](/wiki/Accelerometer "Accelerometer") and [gyroscopes](/wiki/Gyroscope "Gyroscope") enable automatic control of screen rotation. Uses by third-party software include [bubble level](/wiki/Bubble_level "Bubble level") simulation. An [ambient light sensor](/wiki/Ambient_light_sensor "Ambient light sensor") allows for automatic screen brightness and contrast adjustment, and an [RGB sensor](/w/index.php?title=RGB_sensor&action=edit&redlink=1 "RGB sensor (page does not exist)") enables the adaption of screen colour.

Many mobile phones are also equipped with a [barometer](/wiki/Barometer "Barometer") sensor to measure air pressure, such as Samsung since 2012 with the [Galaxy S3](/wiki/Galaxy_S3 "Galaxy S3"), and Apple since 2014 with the [iPhone 6](/wiki/IPhone_6 "IPhone 6"). It allows estimating and detecting changes in altitude.

A [magnetometer](/wiki/Magnetometer "Magnetometer") can act as a [digital compass](/wiki/Digital_magnetic_compass "Digital magnetic compass") by measuring [Earth's magnetic field](/wiki/Earth%27s_magnetic_field "Earth's magnetic field").

#### Rare sensors

Samsung equips their flagship smartphones since the 2014 [Galaxy S5](/wiki/Galaxy_S5 "Galaxy S5") and [Galaxy Note 4](/wiki/Galaxy_Note_4 "Galaxy Note 4") with a [heart rate](/wiki/Heart_rate "Heart rate") sensor to assist in fitness-related uses and act as a shutter key for the front-facing camera.[[30]](#cite_note-30)

So far, only the 2013 [Samsung Galaxy S4](/wiki/Samsung_Galaxy_S4 "Samsung Galaxy S4") and [Note 3](/wiki/Samsung_Galaxy_Note_3 "Samsung Galaxy Note 3") are equipped with an [ambient temperature sensor](/wiki/Thermometer "Thermometer") and a [humidity sensor](/wiki/Hygrometer "Hygrometer"), and only the [Note 4](/wiki/Samsung_Galaxy_Note_4 "Samsung Galaxy Note 4") with an [ultraviolet](/wiki/Ultraviolet "Ultraviolet") radiation sensor which could warn the user about excessive exposure.[[31]](#cite_note-31)[[32]](#cite_note-32)

A rear infrared [laser](/wiki/Laser "Laser") beam for distance measurement can enable [time-of-flight camera](/wiki/Time-of-flight_camera "Time-of-flight camera") functionality with accelerated [autofocus](/wiki/Autofocus "Autofocus"), as implemented on select LG mobile phones starting with [LG G3](/wiki/LG_G3 "LG G3") and [LG V10](/wiki/LG_V10 "LG V10").

Due to their currently rare occurrence among smartphones, not much software to utilize these sensors has been developed yet.

### Storage

While [eMMC](/wiki/EMMC "EMMC") (*embedded multi media card*) [flash storage](/wiki/Flash_storage "Flash storage") was most commonly used in mobile phones, its successor, [UFS](/wiki/Universal_Flash_Storage "Universal Flash Storage") (*Universal Flash Storage*) with higher transfer rates emerged throughout the 2010s for upper-class devices.[[33]](#cite_note-33)

Capacity

While the internal storage capacity of mobile phones has been near-stagnant during the first half of the 2010s, it has increased steeper during its second half, with [Samsung](/wiki/Samsung_Telecommunications "Samsung Telecommunications") for example increasing the available internal storage options of their flagship class units from 32 GB to 512 GB within only 21⁄2 years between 2016 and 2018.[[34]](#cite_note-34)[[35]](#cite_note-35)[[36]](#cite_note-36)[[37]](#cite_note-37)

#### Memory cards

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/05/Huawei_U8950D_no_cover.JPG/250px-Huawei_U8950D_no_cover.JPG)](/wiki/File:Huawei_U8950D_no_cover.JPG)

Inserted memory and SIM cards

Main article: [MicroSD § Mobile phones](/wiki/MicroSD#Mobile_phones "MicroSD")

The space for data storage of some mobile phones can be expanded using [MicroSD](/wiki/MicroSD#Mobile_phones "MicroSD") [memory cards](/wiki/Memory_card "Memory card"), whose capacity has multiplied throughout the 2010s (→ [SD card § 2009–2019: SDXC](/wiki/SD_card#2009–2019:_SDXC "SD card")). Benefits over [USB on the go](/wiki/USB_on_the_go "USB on the go") storage and [cloud storage](/wiki/Cloud_storage "Cloud storage") include [offline](/wiki/Offline "Offline") availability and [privacy](/wiki/Privacy "Privacy"), not reserving and protruding from the [charging port](/w/index.php?title=Charging_port&action=edit&redlink=1 "Charging port (page does not exist)"), no connection instability or [latency](/wiki/Latency_(engineering) "Latency (engineering)"), no dependence on voluminous [data plans](/wiki/Data_plan "Data plan"), and preservation of the limited rewriting cycles of the device's permanent internal storage. Large amounts of data can be moved immediately between devices by changing memory cards, large-scale [data backups](/wiki/Data_backup "Data backup") can be created offline, and data can be read externally should the smartphone be inoperable.[[38]](#cite_note-38)[[39]](#cite_note-39)[[40]](#cite_note-40)

In case of technical [defects](/wiki/Brick_(electronics) "Brick (electronics)") which make the device unusable or [unbootable](/wiki/Boot_(computing) "Boot (computing)") as a result of liquid damage, fall damage, screen damage, [bending damage](/wiki/Bendgate "Bendgate"), [malware](/wiki/Malware "Malware"), or bogus [system updates](/wiki/Software_update "Software update"),[[41]](#cite_note-41) etc., data stored on the memory card is likely [rescueable](/wiki/Data_recovery "Data recovery") externally, while data on the inaccessible internal storage would be [lost](/wiki/Data_loss "Data loss"). A memory card can usually[[a]](#cite_note-42) immediately be re-used in a different memory-card-enabled device with no necessity for prior [file transfers](/wiki/File_transfer "File transfer").

Some [dual-SIM](/wiki/Dual-SIM "Dual-SIM") mobile phones are equipped with a hybrid slot, where one of the two slots can be occupied by either a [SIM card](/wiki/SIM_card "SIM card") or a memory card. Some models, typically of higher end, are equipped with three slots including one dedicated memory card slot, for simultaneous dual-SIM and memory card usage.[[42]](#cite_note-43)

Physical location

The location of both SIM and memory card slots vary among devices, where they might be located accessibly behind the back cover or else behind the battery, the latter of which denies [hot swapping](/wiki/Hot_swapping "Hot swapping").[[43]](#cite_note-44)[[44]](#cite_note-45)

Mobile phones with non-removable rear cover typically house SIM and memory cards in a small tray on the handset's frame, ejected by inserting a needle tool into a pinhole.[[45]](#cite_note-46)

Some earlier mid-range phones such as the 2011 [Samsung Galaxy Fit](/wiki/Samsung_Galaxy_Fit_(smartphone) "Samsung Galaxy Fit (smartphone)") and [Ace](/wiki/Samsung_Galaxy_Ace "Samsung Galaxy Ace") have a sideways memory card slot on the frame covered by a cap that can be opened without tool.[[46]](#cite_note-47)

#### File transfer

Originally, [mass storage](/wiki/Mass_storage "Mass storage") access was commonly enabled to computers through USB. Over time, mass storage access was removed, leaving the [Media Transfer Protocol](/wiki/Media_Transfer_Protocol "Media Transfer Protocol") as protocol for USB file transfer, due to its non-exclusive access ability where the computer is able to access the storage without it being locked away from the mobile phone's software for the duration of the connection, and no necessity for common [file system](/wiki/File_system "File system") support, as communication is done through an [abstraction layer](/wiki/Abstraction_layer "Abstraction layer").

However, unlike mass storage, Media Transfer Protocol lacks parallelism, meaning that only a single transfer can run at a time, for which other transfer requests need to wait to finish. This, for example, denies browsing photos and playing back videos from the device during an active file transfer. Some programs and devices lack support for MTP. In addition, the direct access and [random access](/wiki/Random_access "Random access") of files through MTP is not supported. Any file is wholly downloaded from the device before opened.[[47]](#cite_note-48)

### Sound

Some audio quality enhancing features, such as [Voice over LTE](/wiki/Voice_over_LTE "Voice over LTE") and [HD Voice](/wiki/HD_Voice "HD Voice") have appeared and are often available on newer smartphones. Sound quality can remain a problem due to the design of the phone, the quality of the cellular network and compression algorithms used in [long-distance calls](/wiki/Long-distance_call "Long-distance call").[[48]](#cite_note-49)[[49]](#cite_note-50) Audio quality can be improved using a [VoIP](/wiki/VoIP "VoIP") application over [Wi-Fi](/wiki/Wi-Fi "Wi-Fi").[[50]](#cite_note-51) Cellphones have small speakers so that the user can use a [speakerphone](/wiki/Speakerphone "Speakerphone") feature and talk to a person on the phone without holding it to their ear. The small speakers can also be used to listen to digital audio files of music or speech or watch videos with an audio component, without holding the phone close to the ear. However, integrated speakers may be small and of restricted sound quality to conserve space.

Some mobile phones such as the [HTC One M8](/wiki/HTC_One_M8 "HTC One M8") and the [Sony Xperia Z2](/wiki/Sony_Xperia_Z2 "Sony Xperia Z2") are equipped with [stereophonic](/wiki/Stereophonic_sound "Stereophonic sound") speakers to create spacial sound when in horizontal orientation.[[51]](#cite_note-52)

#### Audio connector

The [3.5mm headphone receptible](/wiki/Phone_connector_(audio)#Miniature_size "Phone connector (audio)") ([coll.](/wiki/Colloquialism "Colloquialism") "*headphone jack*") allows the immediate operation of passive [headphones](/wiki/Headphone "Headphone"), as well as connection to other external auxiliary audio appliances. Among devices equipped with the connector, it is more commonly located at the bottom (charging port side) than on the top of the device.

The decline of the connector's availability among newly released mobile phones among all major vendors commenced in 2016 with its lack on the Apple [iPhone 7](/wiki/IPhone_7 "IPhone 7"). An [adapter](/wiki/Adapter "Adapter") reserving the charging port can retrofit the plug.

Battery-powered, wireless Bluetooth headphones are an alternative. Those tend to be costlier however due to their need for internal hardware such as a Bluetooth [transceiver](/wiki/Transceiver "Transceiver") and a battery with a charging controller, and a Bluetooth coupling is required ahead of each operation.[[52]](#cite_note-53)

### Battery

Smartphones typically feature [lithium-ion](/wiki/Lithium-ion_battery "Lithium-ion battery") or [lithium-polymer batteries](/wiki/Lithium-polymer_battery "Lithium-polymer battery") due to their high [energy densities](/wiki/Energy_density "Energy density").

Batteries chemically wear down as a result of repeated charging and discharging throughout ordinary usage, losing both energy capacity and output power, which results in loss of processing speeds followed by system outages.[[53]](#cite_note-54) Battery capacity may be reduced to 80% after few hundred recharges, and the drop in performance accelerates with time.[[54]](#cite_note-55)[[55]](#cite_note-56)
Some mobile phones are designed with batteries that can be interchanged upon expiration by the end user, usually by opening the back cover. While such a design had initially been used in most mobile phones, including those with touch screen that were not Apple iPhones, it has largely been usurped throughout the 2010s by permanently built-in, non-replaceable batteries; a design practice criticized for [planned obsolescence](/wiki/Planned_obsolescence#Batteries "Planned obsolescence").[[56]](#cite_note-57)

#### Charging

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c0/Cheero_powerbank_%26_Samsung_Galaxy_S4_20140910.jpg/250px-Cheero_powerbank_%26_Samsung_Galaxy_S4_20140910.jpg)](/wiki/File:Cheero_powerbank_%26_Samsung_Galaxy_S4_20140910.jpg)

A high-capacity portable [battery charger](/wiki/Battery_charger "Battery charger") ([*power bank*](/wiki/Battery_charger#Power_bank "Battery charger"))

Due to limitations of [electrical currents](/wiki/Electrical_current "Electrical current") that existing USB cables' copper wires could handle, charging protocols which make use of elevated [voltages](/wiki/Voltages "Voltages") such as [Qualcomm Quick Charge](/wiki/Qualcomm_Quick_Charge "Qualcomm Quick Charge") and [MediaTek Pump Express](/wiki/MediaTek_Pump_Express "MediaTek Pump Express") have been developed to increase the power throughput for faster charging, to maximize the usage time without restricted ergonomy and to minimize the time a device needs to be attached to a power source.

The smartphone's integrated [charge controller](/wiki/Charge_controller "Charge controller") (IC) requests the elevated voltage from a supported [charger](/wiki/Mobile_phone_charger "Mobile phone charger"). "[VOOC](/wiki/VOOC "VOOC")" by Oppo, also marketed as "dash charge", took the counter approach and increased current to cut out some heat produced from internally regulating the arriving voltage in the end device down to the battery's charging terminal voltage, but is incompatible with existing USB cables, as it requires the thicker copper wires of high-current USB cables. Later, [USB Power Delivery](/wiki/USB_Power_Delivery "USB Power Delivery") (*USB-PD*) was developed with the aim to standardize the negotiation of charging parameters across devices of up to 100 Watts, but is only supported on cables with USB-C on both endings due to the connector's dedicated PD channels.[[57]](#cite_note-58)

While charging rates have been increasing, with 15 [watts](/wiki/Watt "Watt") in 2014,[[58]](#cite_note-59) 20 Watts in 2016,[[59]](#cite_note-60) and 45 watts in 2018,[[60]](#cite_note-61) the power throughput may be throttled down significantly during operation of the device.[[61]](#cite_note-62)[[b]](#cite_note-63)

[Wireless charging](/wiki/Wireless_charging "Wireless charging") has been widely adapted, allowing for intermittent recharging without wearing down the charging port through frequent reconnection, with [*Qi*](/wiki/Qi_(standard) "Qi (standard)") being the most common standard, followed by [Powermat](/wiki/Powermat "Powermat"). Due to the lower efficiency of wireless power transmission, charging rates are below that of wired charging, and more heat is produced at similar charging rates.

By the end of 2017, smartphone battery life has become generally adequate;[[62]](#cite_note-64) however, earlier smartphone battery life was poor due to the weak batteries that could not handle the significant power requirements of the smartphones' computer systems and color screens.[[63]](#cite_note-65)[[64]](#cite_note-66)[[65]](#cite_note-67)

Smartphone users purchase additional chargers for use outside the home, at work, and in cars and by buying portable external "battery packs". External battery packs include generic models which are connected to the smartphone with a cable, and custom-made models that "piggyback" onto a smartphone's case. In 2016, Samsung had to recall millions of the [Galaxy Note 7](/wiki/Galaxy_Note_7 "Galaxy Note 7") smartphones due to an explosive battery issue.[[66]](#cite_note-68) For consumer convenience, [wireless charging](/wiki/Inductive_charging "Inductive charging") stations have been introduced in some hotels, bars, and other public spaces.[[67]](#cite_note-69)

#### Power management

A technique to minimize power consumption is the panel self-refresh, whereby the image to be shown on the display is not sent at all times from the processor to the integrated controller (IC) of the display component, but only if the information on screen is changed. The display's integrated controller instead memorizes the last screen contents and refreshes the screen by itself. This technology was introduced around 2014 and has reduced power consumption by a few hundred milliwatts.[[68]](#cite_note-70)

### Cameras

Main articles: [Camera phone](/wiki/Camera_phone "Camera phone"), [Digital camera](/wiki/Digital_camera "Digital camera"), and [Videophone](/wiki/Videophone "Videophone")

Cameras have become standard features of smartphones. As of 2019[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Smartphone&action=edit) phone cameras are now a highly competitive area of differentiation between models, with advertising campaigns commonly based on a focus on the quality or capabilities of a device's main cameras.

Images are usually saved in the [JPEG](/wiki/JPEG "JPEG") file format; some high-end phones since the mid-2010s also have [RAW](/wiki/Raw_image_format "Raw image format") imaging capability.[[69]](#cite_note-71)[[70]](#cite_note-72)

#### Space constraints

Typically smartphones have at least one main rear-facing camera and a lower-resolution front-facing camera for "[selfies](/wiki/Selfie "Selfie")" and [video chat](/wiki/Video_chat "Video chat"). Owing to the limited depth available in smartphones for [image sensors](/wiki/Image_sensor "Image sensor") and [optics](/wiki/Lens "Lens"), rear-facing cameras are often housed in a "bump" that is thicker than the rest of the phone. Since increasingly thin mobile phones have more abundant horizontal space than the depth that is necessary and used in dedicated cameras for better lenses, there is additionally a trend for phone manufacturers to include multiple cameras, with each optimized for a different purpose ([telephoto](/wiki/Telephoto_lens "Telephoto lens"), [wide angle](/wiki/Wide-angle_lens "Wide-angle lens"), etc.).

Viewed from back, rear cameras are commonly located at the top center or top left corner. A cornered location benefits by not requiring other hardware to be packed around the camera module while increasing [ergonomy](/wiki/Ergonomy "Ergonomy"), as the lens is less likely to be covered when held horizontally.

Modern advanced smartphones have cameras with [optical image stabilisation](/wiki/Image_stabilization#Optical_image_stabilization "Image stabilization") (OIS), larger sensors, bright lenses, and even optical zoom plus [RAW](/wiki/Raw_image_format "Raw image format") images. [HDR](/wiki/High-dynamic-range_imaging "High-dynamic-range imaging"), "[Bokeh](/wiki/Bokeh "Bokeh") mode" with multi lenses and multi-shot [night modes](/wiki/Night_mode_camera "Night mode camera") are now also familiar.[[71]](#cite_note-73) Many new smartphone camera features are being enabled via [computational photography](/wiki/Computational_photography "Computational photography") [image processing](/wiki/Digital_image_processing "Digital image processing") and multiple specialized lenses rather than larger sensors and lenses, due to the constrained space available inside phones that are being made as slim as possible.

#### Dedicated camera button

Some mobile phones such as the [Samsung i8000 Omnia 2](/wiki/Samsung_i8000_Omnia_2 "Samsung i8000 Omnia 2"), some [Nokia Lumias](/wiki/Nokia_Lumia "Nokia Lumia") and some [Sony Xperias](/wiki/Sony_Xperia "Sony Xperia") are equipped with a physical camera shutter button.

Those with two pressure levels resemble the [point-and-shoot](/wiki/Point-and-shoot "Point-and-shoot") intuition of dedicated [compact cameras](/wiki/Compact_camera "Compact camera"). The camera button may be used as a [shortcut](/wiki/Shortcut_(computing) "Shortcut (computing)") to quickly and [ergonomically](/wiki/Ergonomy "Ergonomy") launch the camera software, as it is located more accessibly inside a pocket than the power button.

### Back cover materials

Back covers of smartphones are typically made of [polycarbonate](/wiki/Polycarbonate "Polycarbonate"), aluminium, or glass. Polycarbonate back covers may be glossy or matte, and possibly textured, like dotted on the [Galaxy S5](/wiki/Galaxy_S5 "Galaxy S5") or leathered on the [Galaxy Note 3](/wiki/Galaxy_Note_3 "Galaxy Note 3") and [Note 4](/wiki/Note_4 "Note 4").

While polycarbonate back covers may be perceived as less "premium" among [fashion](/wiki/Fashion "Fashion")- and [trend](/wiki/Fad "Fad")-oriented users, its utilitarian strengths and technical benefits include durability and shock absorption, greater [elasticity](/wiki/Elasticity_(physics) "Elasticity (physics)") against permanent bending like metal, inability to shatter like glass, which facilitates designing it removable; better manufacturing cost efficiency, and no blockage of radio signals or wireless power like metal.[[72]](#cite_note-74)[[73]](#cite_note-75)[[74]](#cite_note-76)[[75]](#cite_note-77)

### Accessories

A wide range of accessories are sold for smartphones, including [cases](/wiki/Mobile_phone_accessories#Cases "Mobile phone accessories"), [memory cards](#Memory_cards), [screen protectors](/wiki/Screen_protector "Screen protector"), [chargers](/wiki/Battery_charger#Mobile_phone_charger "Battery charger"), [wireless power](/wiki/Wireless_charging "Wireless charging") stations, [USB On-The-Go](/wiki/USB_On-The-Go "USB On-The-Go") adapters (for connecting USB drives and or, in some cases, a HDMI cable to an external monitor), [MHL](/wiki/Mobile_High-Definition_Link "Mobile High-Definition Link") adapters, add-on batteries, [power banks](/wiki/Power_bank "Power bank"), [headphones](/wiki/Headphones "Headphones"), combined headphone-microphones (which, for example, allow a person to privately conduct [calls](/wiki/Telephone_call "Telephone call") on the device without holding it to the ear), and [Bluetooth](/wiki/Bluetooth "Bluetooth")-enabled [powered speakers](/wiki/Powered_speaker "Powered speaker") that enable users to listen to media from their smartphones wirelessly.

Cases range from relatively inexpensive rubber or soft plastic cases which provide moderate protection from bumps and good protection from scratches to more expensive, heavy-duty cases that combine a rubber padding with a hard outer shell. Some cases have a "book"-like form, with a cover that the user opens to use the device; when the cover is closed, it protects the screen. Some "book"-like cases have additional pockets for credit cards, thus enabling people to use them as [wallets](/wiki/Wallet "Wallet").

Accessories include products sold by the manufacturer of the smartphone and compatible products made by other manufacturers.

However, some companies, like [Apple](/wiki/Apple_Inc. "Apple Inc."), stopped including chargers with smartphones in order to "reduce [carbon footprint](/wiki/Carbon_footprint "Carbon footprint")", etc., causing many customers to pay extra for charging adapters.

## Software

### Mobile operating systems

Main article: [Mobile operating system](/wiki/Mobile_operating_system "Mobile operating system")

A mobile operating system (or mobile OS) is an [operating system](/wiki/Operating_system "Operating system") for phones, [tablets](/wiki/Tablet_computer "Tablet computer"), [smartwatches](/wiki/Smartwatch "Smartwatch"), or other [mobile devices](/wiki/Mobile_device "Mobile device"). Globally, Android and [IOS](/wiki/IOS "IOS") are the two most used mobile operating systems based on [usage share](/wiki/Usage_share_of_operating_systems "Usage share of operating systems"), with the former having been the best selling OS globally on all devices since 2013.

Mobile operating systems combine features of a [personal computer](/wiki/Personal_computer "Personal computer") operating system with other features useful for mobile or handheld use; usually including, and most of the following considered essential in modern mobile systems; a [touchscreen](/wiki/Touchscreen "Touchscreen"), [cellular](/wiki/Cellular_network "Cellular network"), Bluetooth, Wi-Fi Protected Access, Wi-Fi, [Global Positioning System](/wiki/Global_Positioning_System "Global Positioning System") (GPS) mobile navigation, [video-](/wiki/Video_camera "Video camera") and [single-frame picture cameras](/wiki/Digital_camera "Digital camera"), [speech recognition](/wiki/Speech_recognition "Speech recognition"), [voice recorder](/wiki/Sound_recording_and_reproduction "Sound recording and reproduction"), [music player](/wiki/Portable_media_player "Portable media player"), [near-field communication](/wiki/Near-field_communication "Near-field communication"), and [infrared blaster](/wiki/Infrared_blaster "Infrared blaster"). By Q1 2018, over 383 million smartphones were sold with 85.9 percent running Android, 14.1 percent running iOS and a negligible number of smartphones running other OSes.[[76]](#cite_note-78) Android alone is more popular than the popular desktop operating system Windows, and in general, smartphone use (even without tablets) exceeds desktop use. Other well-known mobile operating systems are [Flyme OS](/wiki/Flyme_OS "Flyme OS") and [Harmony OS](/wiki/Harmony_OS "Harmony OS").

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/df/20240419_%EC%82%BC%EC%84%B1_%EA%B0%A4%EB%9F%AD%EC%8B%9C_S24_%EC%9A%B8%ED%8A%B8%EB%9D%BC.jpg/250px-20240419_%EC%82%BC%EC%84%B1_%EA%B0%A4%EB%9F%AD%EC%8B%9C_S24_%EC%9A%B8%ED%8A%B8%EB%9D%BC.jpg)](/wiki/File:20240419_%EC%82%BC%EC%84%B1_%EA%B0%A4%EB%9F%AD%EC%8B%9C_S24_%EC%9A%B8%ED%8A%B8%EB%9D%BC.jpg)

The [Samsung Galaxy S24 Ultra](/wiki/Samsung_Galaxy_S24_Ultra "Samsung Galaxy S24 Ultra") has an [artificial intelligence](/wiki/Artificial_intelligence "Artificial intelligence")-powered feature

Mobile devices with mobile communications abilities (e.g., smartphones) contain two mobile operating systems—the main user-facing software platform is supplemented by a second low-level proprietary [real-time operating system](/wiki/Real-time_operating_system "Real-time operating system") which operates the radio and other hardware. Research has shown that these low-level systems may contain a range of security vulnerabilities permitting malicious [base stations](/wiki/Base_station "Base station") to gain high levels of control over the mobile device.[[77]](#cite_note-79)

### Mobile apps

Main article: [Mobile app](/wiki/Mobile_app "Mobile app")

A mobile app is a computer program designed to run on a mobile device, such as a smartphone. The term "app" is a short-form of the term "software application".[[78]](#cite_note-80)

### Application stores

Main article: [List of mobile app distribution platforms](/wiki/List_of_mobile_app_distribution_platforms "List of mobile app distribution platforms")

The introduction of Apple's App Store for the iPhone and iPod Touch in July 2008 popularized manufacturer-hosted [online distribution](/wiki/List_of_mobile_app_distribution_platforms "List of mobile app distribution platforms") for third-party applications ([software](/wiki/Software "Software") and [computer programs](/wiki/Computer_programs "Computer programs")) focused on a single platform. There are a huge variety of apps, including [video games](/wiki/Video_game "Video game"), music products and business tools. Up until that point, smartphone application distribution depended on [third-party sources](/wiki/List_of_mobile_app_distribution_platforms#Third-party_platforms "List of mobile app distribution platforms") providing applications for multiple platforms, such as [GetJar](/wiki/GetJar "GetJar"), [Handango](/wiki/Handango "Handango"), [Handmark](/wiki/Handmark "Handmark"), and [PocketGear](/wiki/PocketGear "PocketGear"). Following the success of the App Store, other smartphone manufacturers launched application stores, such as Google's Android Market (later renamed to the Google Play Store) and RIM's [BlackBerry App World](/wiki/BlackBerry_App_World "BlackBerry App World"), Android-related app stores like [Aptoide](/wiki/Aptoide "Aptoide"), [Cafe Bazaar](/wiki/Cafe_Bazaar "Cafe Bazaar"), [F-Droid](/wiki/F-Droid "F-Droid"), [GetJar](/wiki/GetJar "GetJar"), and [Opera Mobile Store](/wiki/Opera_Mobile_Store "Opera Mobile Store"). In February 2014, 93% of [mobile developers](/wiki/Mobile_developers "Mobile developers") were targeting smartphones first for mobile app development.[[79]](#cite_note-81)

## Sales

![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/en/timeline/labdynhr81maykmy724bi94vjwk2lyv.png)

Since 1996, smartphone shipments have had positive growth. In November 2011, 27% of all photographs created were taken with camera-equipped smartphones.[[80]](#cite_note-82) In September 2012, a study concluded that 4 out of 5 smartphone owners use the device to shop online.[[81]](#cite_note-83) Global smartphone sales surpassed the sales figures for feature phones in early 2013.[[82]](#cite_note-news1-84) Worldwide shipments of smartphones topped 1 billion units in 2013, up 38% from 2012's 725 million, while comprising a 55% share of the mobile phone market in 2013, up from 42% in 2012. In 2013, smartphone sales began to decline for the first time.[[83]](#cite_note-85)[[84]](#cite_note-86) In Q1 2016 for the first time the shipments dropped by 3 percent [year on year](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/year-over-year "wikt:year-over-year"). The situation was caused by the maturing China market.[[85]](#cite_note-87) A report by NPD shows that fewer than 10% of US citizens have spent $1,000 or more on smartphones, as they are too expensive for most people, without introducing particularly innovative features, and amid [Huawei](/wiki/Huawei "Huawei"), [Oppo](/wiki/Oppo "Oppo") and [Xiaomi](/wiki/Xiaomi "Xiaomi") introducing products with similar feature sets for lower prices.[[86]](#cite_note-88)[[87]](#cite_note-89)[[88]](#cite_note-90) In 2019, smartphone sales declined by 3.2%, the largest in smartphone history, while China and India were credited with driving most smartphone sales worldwide.[[89]](#cite_note-91) It is predicted that widespread adoption of 5G will help drive new smartphone sales.[[90]](#cite_note-92)[[91]](#cite_note-93) It is also predicted that prices of smartphones may go higher due to global [AI](/wiki/Artificial_intelligence "Artificial intelligence") Chip shortage which may effect the sales also.[[92]](#cite_note-94)

### By manufacturer

See also: [List of best-selling mobile phones](/wiki/List_of_best-selling_mobile_phones "List of best-selling mobile phones")

In 2011, [Samsung](/wiki/Samsung "Samsung") had the highest shipment [market share](/wiki/Market_share "Market share") worldwide, followed by [Apple](/wiki/Apple_Inc. "Apple Inc."). In 2013, Samsung had 31.3% market share, a slight increase from 30.3% in 2012, while Apple was at 15.3%, a decrease from 18.7% in 2012. [Huawei](/wiki/Huawei "Huawei"), [LG](/wiki/LG_Corporation "LG Corporation") and [Lenovo](/wiki/Lenovo "Lenovo") were at about 5% each, significantly better than 2012 figures, while others had about 40%, the same as the previous years figure. Only Apple lost market share, although their shipment volume still increased by 12.9%; the rest had significant increases in shipment volumes of 36 to 92%.[[93]](#cite_note-95)

In Q1 2014, Samsung had a 31% share and Apple had 16%.[[94]](#cite_note-96) In Q4 2014, Apple had a 20.4% share and Samsung had 19.9%.[[95]](#cite_note-brett-97) In Q2 2016, Samsung had a 22.3% share and Apple had 12.9%.[[96]](#cite_note-98) In Q1 2017, IDC reported that Samsung was first placed, with 80 million units, followed by Apple with 50.8 million, Huawei with 34.6 million, [Oppo](/wiki/Oppo "Oppo") with 25.5 million and [Vivo](/wiki/Vivo_(technology_company) "Vivo (technology company)") with 22.7 million.[[97]](#cite_note-99)

Samsung's mobile business is half the size of Apple's, by revenue. Apple business increased very rapidly in the years 2013 to 2017.[[98]](#cite_note-100) [Realme](/wiki/Realme "Realme"), a brand owned by Oppo, is the fastest-growing phone brand worldwide since Q2 2019. In China, Huawei and [Honor](/wiki/Honor_(brand) "Honor (brand)"), a brand owned by Huawei, have 46% of market share combined and posted 66% annual growth as of 2019[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Smartphone&action=edit), amid growing Chinese nationalism.[[99]](#cite_note-101) In 2019, Samsung had a 74% market share of 5G smartphones in South Korea.[[100]](#cite_note-102)

In the first quarter of 2024, global smartphone shipments rose by 7.8% to 289.4 million units. Samsung, with a 20.8% market share, overtook Apple to become the leading smartphone manufacturer. Apple's smartphone shipments dropped 10%. Xiaomi secured the third spot with a 14.1% market share.[[101]](#cite_note-103)

The rise in popularity of touchscreen smartphones and mobile apps distributed via app stores along with rapidly advancing [network](/wiki/Cellular_network "Cellular network"), [mobile players](/wiki/Portable_media_player "Portable media player") were replaced by a smartphone as the single device most people sensors](/wiki/Image_sensor "Image sensor") and on-device [image processing](/wiki/Digital_image_processing "Digital image processing") software more gradually led to smartphones replacing simpler [cameras](/wiki/Point-and-shoot_camera "Point-and-shoot camera") for photographs and video recording.[[107]](#cite_note-SmartphonesKillingPoint-and-shoots-109) The built-in [GPS](/wiki/Global_navigation_satellite_systems "Global navigation satellite systems") capabilities and [mapping](/wiki/List_of_online_map_services "List of online map services") apps on smartphones largely replaced stand-alone [satellite navigation](/wiki/Satellite_navigation "Satellite navigation") [devices](/wiki/Personal_navigation_assistant#Market_developments "Personal navigation grew in popularity,[[109]](#cite_note-111) allowing many people to use them in place of [handheld game consoles](/wiki/Handheld_game_consoles "Handheld game consoles"), and some companies tried creating game console/phone hybrids based on phone hardware and software.[[110]](#cite_note-112)[[111]](#cite_note-113) People frequently have chosen not to get [fixed-line telephone service](/wiki/Landline "Landline") in favor of smartphones.[[112]](#cite_note-114)[[113]](#cite_note-115) [Music streaming](/wiki/Streaming_media#Music_streaming_platforms "Streaming media") apps and services have grown rapidly in popularity, serving the same use as listening to music stations on a terrestrial or satellite [radio](/wiki/Radio_receiver "Radio receiver"). [Streaming video](/wiki/Streaming_media "Streaming media") services are easily accessed via smartphone apps and can be used in place of watching [television](/wiki/Television "Television"). People have often stopped wearing [wristwatches](/wiki/Wristwatch "Wristwatch") in favor of checking the time on their smartphones, and many use the clock features on their phones in place of [alarm clocks](/wiki/Alarm_clock "Alarm clock").[[114]](#cite_note-116) Mobile phones can also be used as a digital [note taking](/wiki/Note_taking "Note taking"), [text editing](/wiki/Text_editing "Text editing") and [memorandum](/wiki/Memorandum "Memorandum") device whose [computerization](/wiki/Computer "Computer") facilitates searching of entries.

Additionally, [in many lesser technologically developed regions](/wiki/Global_Internet_usage#Broadband_usage "Global Internet usage") smartphones are people's first and only means of [Internet access](/wiki/Mobile_broadband "Mobile broadband") due to their portability,[[115]](#cite_note-117)[*[failed verification](/wiki/Wikipedia:Verifiability "Wikipedia:Verifiability")*] with [personal computers](/wiki/Personal_computer "Personal computer") being relatively uncommon outside of business use. The cameras on smartphones can be used to photograph documents and send them via email or [messaging](/wiki/Text_messaging "Text messaging") in place of using [fax](/wiki/Fax "Fax") (facsimile) machines. [Payment apps and services](/wiki/Mobile_payment "Mobile payment") on smartphones allow people to make less use of wallets, purses, credit and debit cards, and cash. [Mobile banking](/wiki/Mobile_banking "Mobile banking") apps can allow people to deposit checks simply by photographing them, eliminating the need to take the physical check to an [ATM](/wiki/Automated_teller_machine "Automated teller machine") or teller. [Guide book](/wiki/Guide_book "Guide book") apps can take the place of paper travel and restaurant/business guides, museum brochures, and dedicated [audio guide](/wiki/Audio_tour#Smartphone_tours "Audio tour") equipment.

### Mobile banking and payment

Main articles: [Mobile banking](/wiki/Mobile_banking "Mobile banking") and [Mobile payment](/wiki/Mobile_payment "Mobile payment")

See also: [Branchless banking](/wiki/Branchless_banking "Branchless banking") and [Contactless payment](/wiki/Contactless_payment "Contactless payment")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/22/Mobile_payment_03.JPG/250px-Mobile_payment_03.JPG)](/wiki/File:Mobile_payment_03.JPG)

Mobile payment system.

In many countries, mobile phones are used to provide [mobile banking](/wiki/Mobile_banking "Mobile banking") services, which may include the ability to transfer cash payments by secure SMS text message. Kenya's [M-PESA](/wiki/M-PESA "M-PESA") mobile banking service, for example, allows customers of the mobile phone operator [Safaricom](/wiki/Safaricom "Safaricom") to hold cash balances which are recorded on their SIM cards. Cash can be deposited or withdrawn from M-PESA accounts at Safaricom retail outlets located throughout the country and can be transferred electronically from person to person and used to pay bills to companies.

[Branchless banking](/wiki/Branchless_banking "Branchless banking") has been successful in South Africa and the [Philippines](/wiki/Philippines "Philippines"). A pilot project in [Bali](/wiki/Bali "Bali") was launched in 2011 by the [International Finance Corporation](/wiki/International_Finance_Corporation "International Finance Corporation") and an [Indonesian](/wiki/Indonesia "Indonesia") bank, [Bank Mandiri](/wiki/Bank_Mandiri "Bank Mandiri").[[116]](#cite_note-118)

Another application of mobile banking technology is [Zidisha](/wiki/Zidisha "Zidisha"), a US-based nonprofit micro-lending platform that allows residents of developing countries to raise small business loans from Web users worldwide. Zidisha uses mobile banking for loan disbursements and repayments, transferring funds from lenders in the United States to borrowers in rural Africa who have mobile phones and can use the Internet.[[117]](#cite_note-119)

Mobile payments were first trialled in Finland in 1998 when two Coca-Cola vending machines in [Espoo](/wiki/Espoo "Espoo") were enabled to work with SMS payments. Eventually, the idea spread and in 1999, the Philippines launched the country's first commercial mobile payments systems with mobile operators [Globe](/wiki/Globe_Telecom "Globe Telecom") and [Smart](/wiki/Smart_Communications "Smart Communications").

Some mobile phones can make [mobile payments](/wiki/Mobile_payment "Mobile payment") via direct mobile billing schemes, or through [contactless payments](/wiki/Contactless_payment "Contactless payment") if the phone and the [point of sale](/wiki/Point_of_sale "Point of sale") support [near-field communication](/wiki/Near-field_communication "Near-field communication") (NFC).[[118]](#cite_note-120) Enabling contactless payments through NFC-equipped mobile phones requires the co-operation of manufacturers, network operators, and retail merchants.[[119]](#cite_note-121)[[120]](#cite_note-122)
