Films are increasingly made using smartphones and tablets, leading to the rise of dedicated film festivals for such films, including the [SmartFone Flick Fest](/wiki/SmartFone_Flick_Fest "SmartFone Flick Fest") in [Sydney](/wiki/Sydney "Sydney"), Australia;[[121]](#cite_note-ff-123)[[122]](#cite_note-kornits2024-124) Dublin Smartphone Film Festival; the International Mobil Film Festival based in [San Diego](/wiki/San_Diego "San Diego"); the Spanish festival Cinephone – Festival Internacional de Cine con Smartphone; the African Smartphone International Film Festival;[[123]](#cite_note-125) Toronto Smartphone Film Festival; New York Mobile Film Festival; and others.[[124]](#cite_note-126)

## Criticism and issues

### Social impacts

#### Manufacture

[Cobalt](/wiki/Cobalt "Cobalt") and [lithium](/wiki/Lithium "Lithium") are needed in order to manufacture smartphones' rechargeable batteries. Workers in cobalt and lithium mining, including children, suffer injuries, amputations, and death as the result of the hazardous working conditions and mine tunnel collapses in the [Democratic Republic of the Congo](/wiki/Democratic_Republic_of_the_Congo "Democratic Republic of the Congo") during [artisanal mining of cobalt](/wiki/Cobalt#Democratic_Republic_of_the_Congo "Cobalt").[[125]](#cite_note-127)[[126]](#cite_note-128) Reports indicate that thousands of artisanal lithium diggers are working in unsafe conditions, with reports of child labour and miners being buried by a mine collapse, also in [Zimbabwe](/wiki/Zimbabwe "Zimbabwe"); and suspected corruption cases in [Namibia](/wiki/Namibia "Namibia") and the Democratic Republic of the Congo. In 2019 [a lawsuit](/wiki/International_Rights_Advocates_v._Apple,_Microsoft,_Dell,_Tesla "International Rights Advocates v. Apple, Microsoft, Dell, Tesla") was filed against Apple and other tech companies for the use of [child labor](/wiki/Child_labor "Child labor") in mining cobalt;[[127]](#cite_note-129)[[128]](#cite_note-130) in 2024 the court ruled that the companies were not liable.[[129]](#cite_note-131) Apple announced it would convert to using recycled cobalt by 2025.[[130]](#cite_note-132)

#### Use

Main article: [Problematic smartphone use](/wiki/Problematic_smartphone_use "Problematic smartphone use")

In 2012, [University of Southern California](/wiki/University_of_Southern_California "University of Southern California") study found that [unprotected](/wiki/Safe_sex "Safe sex") [adolescent sexual](/wiki/Adolescent_sexuality "Adolescent sexuality") activity was more common among owners of smartphones.[[131]](#cite_note-133)

A study conducted by the [Rensselaer Polytechnic Institute](/wiki/Rensselaer_Polytechnic_Institute "Rensselaer Polytechnic Institute")'s (RPI) Lighting Research Center (LRC) concluded that smartphones, or any backlit devices, can seriously affect [sleep cycles](/wiki/Stages_of_sleep "Stages of sleep").[[132]](#cite_note-Colaner-134)

Some persons might become psychologically attached to smartphones, resulting in anxiety when separated from the devices.[[133]](#cite_note-135)

A "[smombie](/wiki/Smombie "Smombie")" (a combination of "smartphone" and "[zombie](/wiki/Zombie "Zombie")") is a walking person using a smartphone and not paying attention as they walk, possibly risking an accident in the process, an increasing social phenomenon.[[134]](#cite_note-sunday-times-136) The issue of slow-moving smartphone users led to the temporary creation of a "mobile lane" for walking in [Chongqing](/wiki/Chongqing "Chongqing"), China.[[135]](#cite_note-bbc-137) The issue of distracted smartphone users led the city of [Augsburg](/wiki/Augsburg "Augsburg"), Germany, to embed pedestrian traffic lights in the pavement.[[136]](#cite_note-138)

### While driving

Main articles: [Mobile phones and driving safety](/wiki/Mobile_phones_and_driving_safety "Mobile phones and driving safety") and [Texting while driving](/wiki/Texting_while_driving "Texting while driving")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/4d/Hand_held_phones.JPG/250px-Hand_held_phones.JPG)](/wiki/File:Hand_held_phones.JPG)

A [New York City](/wiki/New_York_City "New York City") driver holding two phones

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/03/Range_Rover_mapping_app.jpg/250px-Range_Rover_mapping_app.jpg)](/wiki/File:Range_Rover_mapping_app.jpg)

A user consulting a mapping app on a phone

Mobile phone use while driving—including [calling](/wiki/Telephone_call "Telephone call"), [text messaging](/wiki/Text_messaging "Text messaging"), playing media, [web browsing](/wiki/Web_browsing "Web browsing"), [gaming](/wiki/Video_game "Video game"), using mapping apps or operating other phone features—is common but controversial, since it is widely considered dangerous due to what is known as [distracted driving](/wiki/Distracted_driving "Distracted driving"). Being distracted while operating a motor vehicle has been shown to increase the risk of [accidents](/wiki/Accident "Accident"). In September 2010, the US [National Highway Traffic Safety Administration](/wiki/National_Highway_Traffic_Safety_Administration "National Highway Traffic Safety Administration") (NHTSA) reported that 995 people were killed by drivers distracted by phones. In March 2011 a US insurance company, [State Farm Insurance](/wiki/State_Farm_Insurance "State Farm Insurance"), announced the results of a study which showed 19% of drivers surveyed accessed the Internet on a smartphone while driving.[[137]](#cite_note-post-139) Many jurisdictions prohibit the use of mobile phones while driving. In Egypt, Israel, Japan, Portugal and Singapore, both handheld and hands-free [calling](/wiki/Telephone_call "Telephone call") on a mobile phone (which uses a [speakerphone](/wiki/Speakerphone "Speakerphone")) is banned. In other countries, including the UK and France, and in many US states, calling is only banned on handheld phones, while hands-free calling is permitted.

A 2011 study reported that over 90% of college students surveyed text (initiate, reply or read) while driving.[[138]](#cite_note-140)
The [scientific literature](/wiki/Scientific_literature "Scientific literature") on the danger of driving while sending a text message from a mobile phone, or [texting while driving](/wiki/Texting_while_driving "Texting while driving"), is limited. A simulation study at the [University of Utah](/wiki/University_of_Utah "University of Utah") found a sixfold increase in distraction-related accidents when texting.[[139]](#cite_note-141) Due to the complexity of smartphones that began to grow more after, this has introduced additional difficulties for law enforcement officials when attempting to distinguish one usage from another in drivers using their devices. This is more apparent in countries which ban both handheld and hands-free usage, rather than those which ban handheld use only, as officials cannot easily tell which function of the phone is being used simply by looking at the driver. This can lead to drivers being stopped for using their device illegally for a call when, in fact, they were using the device legally, for example, when using the phone's incorporated controls for car stereo, [GPS](/wiki/GPS "GPS") or [satnav](/wiki/Satnav "Satnav").

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/cd/NocellphonesSouthsidePlaceTX.JPG/250px-NocellphonesSouthsidePlaceTX.JPG)](/wiki/File:NocellphonesSouthsidePlaceTX.JPG)

A sign along [Bellaire Boulevard](/wiki/Bellaire_Boulevard "Bellaire Boulevard") in [Southside Place, Texas](/wiki/Southside_Place,_Texas "Southside Place, Texas") ([Greater Houston](/wiki/Greater_Houston "Greater Houston")) states that using mobile phones while driving is prohibited from 7:30 am to 9:00 am and from 2:00 pm to 4:15 pm.

A 2010 study reviewed the incidence of phone use while [cycling](/wiki/Cycling "Cycling") and its effects on behavior and safety.[[140]](#cite_note-142) In 2013 a national survey in the US reported the number of drivers who reported using their phones to access the Internet while driving had risen to nearly one of four.[[141]](#cite_note-143) A study conducted by the University of Vienna examined approaches for reducing inappropriate and problematic use of mobile phones, such as using phones while driving.[[142]](#cite_note-144)

Accidents involving a driver being distracted by being in a [call](/wiki/Telephone_call "Telephone call") on a phone have begun to be prosecuted as negligence similar to speeding. In the [United Kingdom](/wiki/United_Kingdom "United Kingdom"), from 27 February 2007, motorists who are caught using a handheld phone while driving will have three penalty points added to their license in addition to the fine of £60.[[143]](#cite_note-BBC-145) This increase was introduced to try to stem the increase in drivers ignoring the law.[[144]](#cite_note-BBC2-146) [Japan](/wiki/Japan "Japan") prohibits all use of phones while driving, including use of hands-free devices. New Zealand has banned handheld phone use since 1 November 2009. Many states in the United States have banned text messaging on phones while driving. Illinois became the 17th American state to enforce this law.[[145]](#cite_note-CNN-147) As of July 2010[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Smartphone&action=edit), 30 states had banned texting while driving, with Kentucky becoming the most recent addition on July 15.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

Public Health Law Research maintains a list of distracted driving laws in the [United States](/wiki/United_States "United States"). This database of laws provides a comprehensive view of the provisions of laws that restrict the use of mobile devices while driving for all 50 states and the District of Columbia between 1992, when first law was passed through December 1, 2010. The dataset contains information on 22 dichotomous, continuous or categorical variables including, for example, activities regulated (e.g., texting versus talking, hands-free versus handheld calls, web browsing, gaming), targeted populations, and exemptions.[[146]](#cite_note-148)

### Legal

Main article: [Smartphone wars](/wiki/Smartphone_wars "Smartphone wars")

A "patent war" between Samsung and Apple started when the latter claimed that the original [Galaxy S](/wiki/Samsung_Galaxy_S_(2010_smartphone) "Samsung Galaxy S (2010 smartphone)") Android phone copied the interface‍—‌and possibly the hardware‍—‌of Apple's iOS for the [iPhone 3GS](/wiki/IPhone_3GS "IPhone 3GS"). There was also smartphone patents licensing and litigation involving [Sony Mobile](/wiki/Sony_Mobile "Sony Mobile"), [Google](/wiki/Google "Google"), [Apple Inc.](/wiki/Apple_Inc. "Apple Inc."), [Samsung](/wiki/Samsung_Electronics "Samsung Electronics"), [Microsoft](/wiki/Microsoft "Microsoft"), [Nokia](/wiki/Nokia "Nokia"), [Motorola](/wiki/Motorola "Motorola"), [HTC](/wiki/HTC "HTC"), [Huawei](/wiki/Huawei "Huawei") and [ZTE](/wiki/ZTE "ZTE"), among others. The conflict is part of the [wider "patent wars"](/wiki/Technology_patent_wars "Technology patent wars") between multinational technology and software corporations. To secure and increase [market share](/wiki/Market_share "Market share"), companies granted a [patent](/wiki/Patent "Patent") can sue to prevent competitors from using the methods the patent covers. Since the 2010s the number of lawsuits, counter-suits, and trade complaints based on patents and [designs](/wiki/Industrial_design_right "Industrial design right") in the market for smartphones, and devices based on smartphone operating systems such as Android and iOS, has increased significantly. Initial suits, countersuits, rulings, license agreements, and other major events began in 2009 as the smartphone market stated to grow more rapidly by 2012.

### Medical

With the rise in number of mobile medical apps in the market place, government regulatory agencies raised concerns on the safety of the use of such applications. These concerns were transformed into regulation initiatives worldwide with the aim of safeguarding users from untrusted medical advice.[[147]](#cite_note-149) According to the findings of these medical experts in recent years, excessive smartphone use in society may lead to headaches, sleep disorders and insufficient sleep, while severe smartphone addiction may lead to physical health problems, such as hunchback, muscle relaxation and uneven nutrition.[[148]](#cite_note-150)

### Impacts on cognition and mental health

Main article: [Digital media use and mental health](/wiki/Digital_media_use_and_mental_health "Digital media use and mental health")

There is a debate about beneficial and detrimental impacts of smartphones or smartphone-uses on cognition and mental health.

### Security

Main articles: [Mobile security](/wiki/Mobile_security "Mobile security"), [Malware](/wiki/Malware "Malware"), [Mobile virus](/wiki/Mobile_virus "Mobile virus"), and [Media Transfer Protocol](/wiki/Media_Transfer_Protocol "Media Transfer Protocol")

Smartphone malware is easily distributed through an insecure app store.[[149]](#cite_note-Juniper-malware-151)[[150]](#cite_note-trustbus11-152) Often, malware is hidden in [pirated](/wiki/Copyright_infringement_of_software "Copyright infringement of software") versions of legitimate apps, which are then distributed through third-party app stores.[[151]](#cite_note-153)[[152]](#cite_note-154) Malware risk also comes from what is known as an "update attack", where a legitimate application is later changed to include a malware component, which users then install when they are notified that the app has been updated.[[153]](#cite_note-Lookout-malware-155) As well, one out of three robberies in 2012 in the United States involved the theft of a mobile phone. An online petition has urged smartphone makers to install [kill switches](/wiki/Kill_switch "Kill switch") in their devices.[[154]](#cite_note-156) In 2014, Apple's "Find my iPhone" and Google's "Android Device Manager" can locate, disable, and wipe the data from phones that have been lost or stolen. With BlackBerry Protect in OS version 10.3.2, devices can be rendered unrecoverable to even BlackBerry's own Operating System recovery tools if incorrectly authenticated or dissociated from their account.[[155]](#cite_note-157)

Leaked documents from 2013 to 2016 codenamed [Vault 7](/wiki/Vault_7 "Vault 7") detail the capabilities of the [United States](/wiki/United_States "United States") [Central Intelligence Agency](/wiki/Central_Intelligence_Agency "Central Intelligence Agency") (CIA) to perform electronic surveillance and [cyber warfare](/wiki/Cyber_warfare "Cyber warfare"), including the ability to compromise the operating systems of most smartphones (including iOS and Android).[[156]](#cite_note-158)[[157]](#cite_note-159) In 2021, journalists and researchers reported the discovery of [spyware](/wiki/Spyware "Spyware"), called [Pegasus](/wiki/Pegasus_(spyware) "Pegasus (spyware)"), developed and distributed by a private company which can and has been used to infect iOS and Android smartphones often—partly via use of [0-day exploits](/wiki/Zero-day_(computing) "Zero-day (computing)")—without the need for any user-interaction or significant clues to the user and then be used to exfiltrate data, track user locations, capture film through its camera, and activate the microphone at any time.[[158]](#cite_note-160) [Analysis of data traffic](/wiki/Packet_analysis "Packet analysis") by popular smartphones running variants of Android found substantial by-default data collection and sharing with no opt-out by this [pre-installed software](/wiki/Pre-installed_software "Pre-installed software").[[159]](#cite_note-161)[[160]](#cite_note-162)

Guidelines for mobile device security were issued by NIST[[161]](#cite_note-163) and many other organizations. For conducting a private, in-person meeting, at least one site recommends that the user switch the smartphone off and disconnect the battery.[[162]](#cite_note-164)

### Sleep

Main article: [Electronic media and sleep](/wiki/Electronic_media_and_sleep "Electronic media and sleep")

Using smartphones late at night can disturb sleep, due to the blue light and brightly lit screen, which affects [melatonin](/wiki/Melatonin "Melatonin") levels and [sleep cycles](/wiki/Sleep_cycles "Sleep cycles"). In an effort to alleviate these issues, "Night Mode" functionality to change the [color temperature](/wiki/Color_temperature "Color temperature") of a screen to a warmer hue based on the time of day to reduce the amount of blue light generated became available through several apps for Android and the [f.lux](/wiki/F.lux "F.lux") software for [jailbroken](/wiki/Jailbreak_(iPhone_OS) "Jailbreak (iPhone OS)") iPhones.[[163]](#cite_note-165) [iOS 9.3](/wiki/IOS_9 "IOS 9") integrated a similar, system-level feature known as "[Night Shift](/wiki/Night_Shift_(software) "Night Shift (software)")". Several Android device manufacturers bypassed Google's initial reluctance to make Night Mode a standard feature in Android and included software for it on their hardware under varying names, before [Android Oreo](/wiki/Android_Oreo "Android Oreo") added it to the OS for compatible devices.[[164]](#cite_note-166)

It has also been theorized that for some users, addiction to use of their phones, especially before they go to bed, can result in "[ego depletion](/wiki/Ego_depletion "Ego depletion")". Many people also use their phones as alarm clocks, which can also lead to loss of sleep.[[165]](#cite_note-cnet-bluelight-167)[[166]](#cite_note-168)[[167]](#cite_note-169)[[168]](#cite_note-170)[[169]](#cite_note-171)

## Restrictions and bans

Further information: [Digital detox](/wiki/Digital_detox "Digital detox")

In some countries authorities make efforts to reduce digital device use, including smartphones among students.

* [South Korea](/wiki/South_Korea "South Korea") passed nationwide classroom phone ban. The law will come to effect in March 2026. Exceptions allowed for students with disabilities, emergencies and educational purposes.

* [France](/wiki/France "France") and [Finland](/wiki/Finland "Finland") made partial bans, generally for younger students.

* [Italy](/wiki/Italy "Italy"), the [Netherlands](/wiki/Netherlands "Netherlands"), and [China](/wiki/China "China") created stronger restrictions. The policy improved the situation in Dutch schools.

* In [Australia](/wiki/Australia "Australia") there are state-level bans. [Victoria](/wiki/Victoria_(state) "Victoria (state)") and [New South Wales](/wiki/New_South_Wales "New South Wales") are introducing policies that prohibit phone use during school hours.

"There is significant scientific and medical proof that smartphone addiction has extremely harmful effects on students' brain development and emotional growth," [Cho Jung-hun](/wiki/Cho_Jung-hun "Cho Jung-hun"), who introduced the bill in South Korea told the BBC. Not all students agree this will solve the problem. "Rather than simply taking phones away, I think the first step should be teaching students what they can do without them," said Seo Min-joon, an 18-year-old high schooler. Another student, aged 13, said that he does not have time to be addicted to his phone due to an overloaded schedule.[[170]](#cite_note-172)

In 2024–2025 Australia and France began to advance legislation which prohibits entirely the use of social media by children under the age of 15–16.[[171]](#cite_note-173) In Australia the law will come into effect from 2026.[[172]](#cite_note-174)

## Replacement of dedicated digital cameras

As the 2010s decade commenced, the sale figures of dedicated compact cameras decreased sharply since mobile phone cameras were increasingly perceived as serving as a sufficient surrogate camera.[[173]](#cite_note-175)

Increases in computing power in mobile phones enabled fast image processing and high-resolution filming, with 1080p Full HD being achieved in 2011 and the barrier to 2160p 4K being breached in 2013.

However, due to design and space limitations, smartphones lack several features found even on low-budget compact cameras, including a [hot-swappable](/wiki/Hot_swapping "Hot swapping") memory card and battery for nearly uninterrupted operation, physical buttons and knobs for focusing and capturing and zooming, a [bolt thread tripod mount](/wiki/Tripod_(photography)#Bolt_threads "Tripod (photography)"), a [capacitor](/wiki/Capacitor "Capacitor")-charged [xenon flash](/wiki/Xenon_flash "Xenon flash") that exceeds the brightness of smartphones' LED flashlights, and an ergonomic grip for steadier holding during handheld shooting, which enables longer exposure times. Since dedicated cameras can be more spacious, they can house larger image sensors and feature [optical zooming](/wiki/Optical_zoom "Optical zoom").

Since the late 2010s, smartphone manufacturers have bypassed the lack of optical zoom to a limited extent by incorporating additional rear cameras with fixed magnification levels.[[174]](#cite_note-176)[[175]](#cite_note-177)

## Lifespan

Further information: [Lithium-ion battery § Lifespan](/wiki/Lithium-ion_battery#Lifespan "Lithium-ion battery"), and [Planned obsolescence § Batteries](/wiki/Planned_obsolescence#Batteries "Planned obsolescence")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/74/Day_4_Agbogloshie_%2825891029818%29.jpg/250px-Day_4_Agbogloshie_%2825891029818%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Day_4_Agbogloshie_(25891029818).jpg)

[E-waste](/wiki/Electronic_waste "Electronic waste") in [Agbogbloshie](/wiki/Agbogbloshie "Agbogbloshie")

In mobile phones released since the second half of the 2010s, operational life span commonly is limited by built-in batteries which are not designed to be interchangeable. The life expectancy of batteries depends on usage intensity of the powered device, where activity (longer usage) and tasks demanding more energy expire the battery earlier.

[Lithium-ion](/wiki/Lithium-ion_battery "Lithium-ion battery") and [lithium-polymer](/wiki/Lithium_polymer_battery "Lithium polymer battery") batteries, those commonly powering [portable electronics](/wiki/Mobile_computing#Portable_computing_devices "Mobile computing"), additionally wear down more from fuller charge and deeper discharge cycles, and when unused for an extended amount of time while depleted, where self-discharging may lead to a harmful depth of discharge.[[176]](#cite_note-Cycle-life-178)[[177]](#cite_note-179)[[178]](#cite_note-180)

Manufacturers have prevented some smartphones from operating after repairs, by associating components' unique serial numbers to the device so it will refuse to operate or disable some functionality in case of a mismatch that would occur after a replacement. Locking of the serial number was first documented in 2015 on the [iPhone 6](/wiki/IPhone_6 "IPhone 6"), which would become inoperable from a detected replacement of the "home" button. Later, some functionality was restricted on Apple and Samsung smartphones when a battery replacement not authorized by the vendor was detected.[[179]](#cite_note-181)[[180]](#cite_note-182)
